elements.sent-2411-002: Sufficient conditions are given for a function $F(p)$ to be the Laplace transform of a function $f(t)$ or a distribution $f$.
elements.sent-2411-003: No assumption on $f$ is given a priori.
elements.sent-2411-004: It is not even assumed that $f=0$ for $t<0$.
elements.sent-2411-005: In reference book \cite{BE one finds tables of the Laplace transform and \cite{S is a text on this transform.}}
elements.sent-2411-006: In \cite{H the theory of the Fourier transform can be found.}
elements.sent-2411-007: It is often important to know if a function $F(p)$ is a Laplace transform of a function or a distribution, see, for example, \cite{R723 .}
elements.sent-2411-008: In the literature it is usually assumed that $f(t)$ satifies some assumptions, for example, $f(t)=0$ for $t<0$, $|f(t)| < ce^{at}$, $a=const\ge 0$, see, for example, \cite{S .}
elements.sent-2411-010: In this paper we do not make any assumptions on $f$.

elements.sent-2411-025: The question of practical interest is: can one prove that the solution $h=h(t)$ is a uniformly bounded function on $\R_+$ and $h(0)=0$? Theorem 1 and Lemma 1, proved below in item c), allow to answer both questions.

elements.sent-2411-048: Consider the following result of the type of a Paley-Wiener (PW) theorem, see \cite{R , p.405.. {\bf Proposition 1.}}
